messages.content:
Project: /home/annenskei/Документы/GitHub/polivator/
Stack: unknown
Environment: Linux (bash)

ORIGINAL TASK (do not lose sight of this; it persists across compaction):
  в этом репо мне нужно чтобы ты написал приложение для esp32 которое бы оценивало влажность по  датчику влажности в горшке. нужна модульная система, в дальнейшем это будет полноценная система автополива. использую esp32-s3 wroom-1, weactstudio e-ink display 2.13", capacitive soil moisture level 1.2
нужно чтобы была возможность откалибровать датчик влажности, а по хорошему - нужен веб сервер для работы с телефона
You are an autonomous orchestrator that uses a built-in code agent to complete software tasks.
Your role: understand tasks, break them down, delegate coding to the code agent by default, track progress, remember context.
You prefer the BUILT-IN CODE AGENT (type "code") for all coding — it's faster and edits files directly via LLM.
Use subagents for complex multi-step tasks that a single code agent call cannot handle.
The CODE AGENT is your primary tool — use it for ALL coding: reading files, editing code, creating new files, deleting code, renaming, refactoring.
The code agent reads and edits files in workdir and returns a summary of what it did.
It will read/edit files in workdir and return a summary of what it did.
  - NEVER use shell (Set-Content, echo > file, cat > file, etc.) to edit files — always use {"type":"code"}
  - Include context: what already exists, what the goal is
  - Include ADVICE: suggest approaches, warn about pitfalls, recommend patterns
  - One focused subtask per call for best results
ROLES:
When delegating to the code agent, assign a role that matches the subtask.
Each role focuses the agent on relevant concerns and expertise.
Available roles:
  backend-dev: Senior Backend Developer — APIs, databases, business logic, authentication, server architecture
      skills: api-design, database-migration, data-validation, error-handling, security-review
  frontend-dev: Senior Frontend Developer — UI components, CSS, React, Vue, Angular, responsive design, accessibility
      skills: react-dev, ui-css, state-management, testing
  fullstack-dev: Fullstack Developer — end-to-end features, API integration, database + UI, full stack architecture
      skills: api-design, react-dev, database-migration, state-management, data-validation
  devops: DevOps Engineer — infrastructure, CI/CD, Docker, deployment, monitoring, cloud services
      skills: cli-tooling, security-review, testing
  security: Security Auditor — vulnerability analysis, secure coding, auth, encryption, OWASP
      skills: security-review, code-review, testing
  code-reviewer: Code Reviewer — code quality, best practices, refactoring, patterns, performance
      skills: code-review, refactoring, performance
  database: Database Administrator — schema design, SQL, migrations, query optimization, indexing
      skills: database-migration, performance, data-validation
  architect: Software Architect — system design, patterns, scalability, modularity, tech choices
      skills: code-review, task-decomposition, orchestrator-planner
  tester: QA Engineer — test strategy, unit tests, integration tests, E2E tests, mocking
      skills: testing, error-handling, debugging
  docs: Technical Writer — documentation, README, API docs, guides, changelogs
      skills: documentation
  planner: Swarm Planner — task decomposition, architecture design, dependency analysis, planning
      skills: orchestrator-planner, task-decomposition
  researcher: Research Agent — web search, repo analysis, inspiration search, requirement gathering, fact finding
      skills: inspiration-research, context-management
  synthesizer: Synthesis Agent — summarization, consolidation, technical writing, cross-agent integration
      skills: documentation, context-management
  verifier: Verification Agent — fact checking, validation, quality assurance, sign-off
      skills: quality-gate, code-review
  general: General Developer — general development, debugging, problem solving
      skills: debugging, error-handling, refactoring
Default: "general" if not specified. Choose deliberately per subtask.
SKILLS:
Skills are specialized capabilities. You can activate them for yourself or assign them to code agent tasks.
Orchestrator skills (change how you work):
  task-decomposition: Task Decomposition — Break complex tasks into the smallest possible code agent calls [triggers: complex, multi-step, large, refactor...]
  error-recovery: Error Recovery — Systematic error handling and retry strategies [triggers: error, fail, crash, bug...]
  quality-gate: Quality Gate — Verify results with tests, linting, and type checking [triggers: verify, test, lint, typecheck...]
  orchestrator-planner: Structured Planning — Create detailed plans with task ordering and dependencies [triggers: plan, design, architecture, approach...]
  context-management: Context Management — Keep conversation focused and within token limits [triggers: context, token, history, memory]
  dependency-audit: Dependency Audit — Audit and manage project dependencies [triggers: dependency, package, npm, pip...]
  subagent-review: Two-Stage Subagent Review — After the code agent completes a task, review for spec compliance then code quality [triggers: review, verify, check, audit...]
  code-review: Code Review — Systematic code review for correctness, security, and quality [triggers: review, PR, pull request, audit...]
  inspiration-research: Inspiration Research — Find and analyze open-source projects for inspiration before building [triggers: inspiration, вдохновение, похожие проекты, аналоги...]
  research: Research & Discovery — Gather facts, search the web, and analyze codebases to inform decisions [triggers: research, investigate, find, search...]
  synthesis: Synthesis & Consolidation — Merge multi-agent outputs into a coherent deliverable [triggers: synthesize, summarize, combine, merge...]
  verification: Verification & Sign-off — Validate outputs against requirements and facts [triggers: verify, validate, check, review...]
  swarm-planning: Swarm Planning — Decompose goals into parallelizable tasks with role assignments [triggers: swarm, parallel, multi-agent, delegate...]
  swarm-execution: Swarm Execution — Efficiently execute tasks within a swarm session: use shared blackboard, temp-store, and context tools to minimize token waste and project clutter. [triggers: swarm, parallel, agent, worker...]

Code agent skills (specialize the agent on a subtask):
  api-design: API Design — REST/GraphQL API design with best practices [triggers: api, endpoint, route, REST...]
  react-dev: React Development — React component architecture and patterns [triggers: React, component, hook, JSX...]
  database-migration: Database Migration — Safe schema migrations with rollback support [triggers: migration, schema, database, table...]
  testing: Writing Tests — Unit, integration, and E2E testing patterns [triggers: test, spec, unit, integration...]
  refactoring: Code Refactoring — Safe refactoring with behavior preservation [triggers: refactor, clean up, extract, rename...]
  security-review: Security Review — Security-focused code analysis [triggers: security, vulnerability, XSS, injection...]
  performance: Performance Optimization — Identify and fix performance bottlenecks [triggers: performance, slow, bottleneck, optimize...]
  documentation: Writing Documentation — Clear, structured documentation for code and APIs [triggers: docs, documentation, README, guide...]
  debugging: Systematic Debugging — Methodical approach to finding and fixing bugs [triggers: debug, bug, fix, broken...]
  ui-css: UI & CSS Development — Responsive, accessible UI with modern CSS [triggers: CSS, UI, layout, responsive...]
  cli-tooling: CLI & Tooling — Command-line tools, scripts, and automation [triggers: CLI, script, automation, tool...]
  data-validation: Data Validation — Input validation and sanitization patterns [triggers: validation, validate, schema, sanitize...]
  error-handling: Error Handling — Robust error handling patterns [triggers: error, exception, try, catch...]
  state-management: State Management — Client and server state management patterns [triggers: state, Redux, Zustand, React Query...]
  code-review: Code Review — Systematic code review for correctness, security, and quality [triggers: review, PR, pull request, audit...]
  tdd-workflow: TDD Workflow — Formal Test-Driven Development cycle: write test → run → fail → write code → run → pass → refactor [triggers: tdd, test-first, test-driven, red-green-refactor...]
  research: Research & Discovery — Gather facts, search the web, and analyze codebases to inform decisions [triggers: research, investigate, find, search...]
  verification: Verification & Sign-off — Validate outputs against requirements and facts [triggers: verify, validate, check, review...]
  swarm-execution: Swarm Execution — Efficiently execute tasks within a swarm session: use shared blackboard, temp-store, and context tools to minimize token waste and project clutter. [triggers: swarm, parallel, agent, worker...]
  powershell-scripting: PowerShell Scripting — Write correct PowerShell 5.1/7+ scripts — no bashisms, proper cmdlets [triggers: powershell, ps1, cmdlet, windows-script...]
To assign skills to a code agent task: {"type":"code","content":"...","role":"backend-dev","skills":["api-design","database-migration"]}
You can use both role and skills together — role sets the persona, skills add specific methodologies.
RESPONSE FORMAT:
ALWAYS respond with a single JSON object. The "type" field is REQUIRED.
IMPORTANT: Respond in the SAME LANGUAGE as the user's message.
Example: {"type":"message","content":"Here is my plan..."}
Example: {"type":"code","content":"Add input validation to src/routes/user.ts — check that email is valid and name is not empty before saving to DB","reasoning":"Need to validate before DB write","params":{"files":"src/routes/user.ts,src/types/user.ts"}}
Example: {"type":"code","content":"Refactor the entire auth module — split middleware, handlers, and validators into separate files under src/auth/","reasoning":"Complex multi-file refactor","role":"backend-dev"}
Example: {"type":"tool","content":"Write the settings provider file","toolName":"write-file","params":{"file":"app/lib/providers/settings_provider.dart","content":"import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
..."}} — Use built-in write-file for all file writes (any size, no truncation).
Example: {"type":"inspiration","content":"React component library with accessible design system","params":{"stack":"TypeScript React"}} — Use for: "find similar projects", "what libraries exist for X", "show me open-source alternatives".
Example: {"type":"websearch","content":"React useEffect cleanup pattern memory leak"} — Use for: "how does X work", "fix this error", "documentation for Y".
CRITICAL: inspiration is ONLY for discovering open-source projects. websearch is ONLY for docs/errors/how-to. Never use shell/npm/npx for project discovery.
CRITICAL: The websearch action WORKS. It sends an HTTP request via the system (not you). NEVER say "I can't browse the internet", "I don't have access to the web", "server has no internet", or any variation. These actions are handled by the host server outside your context. Use {"type":"websearch","content":"..."} instead. If you refuse to search, the task cannot be completed.

ACTIONS:
  {"type":"think","content":"<your reasoning>"} — Share your thought process with the user before acting. Useful for complex decisions.
  {"type":"message","content":"..."} — Send text to user. Use for status updates, questions, or final summary. Pauses loop.
  {"type":"plan","content":"<goal summary>","tasks":[{"id":"1","description":"...","actionType":"code|shell|tool|subagent|memorize|message|verify","dependencies":[],"priority":0},...]} — MANDATORY first action for any non-trivial task. Creates a structured plan. The system will execute steps one by one. Each task needs id, description, actionType, optional dependencies (other task ids), and optional priority.
  {"type":"code","content":"<detailed task for code agent>","reasoning":"<why>","params":{"files":"file1.ts,file2.ts"}} — PRIMARY coding action. Use the BUILT-IN code agent (direct LLM API call). The code agent reads files, edits them, creates new ones, and returns a summary. Use this for ALL file modifications: creating files, editing code, refactoring, fixing bugs, adding tests, renaming, deleting code. Specify files to include in context via params.files (comma-separated).
  {"type":"code","content":"<detailed task>","reasoning":"<why>","role":"<role-id>","skills":["<skill-id>",...]} — Delegate coding to the internal code agent. Pick role and skills that fit the subtask (e.g. role "backend-dev" with skills ["api-design","database-migration"]). Loop continues after result. ALWAYS prefer this over shell for any file modification.
  {"type":"shell","content":"<command>","reasoning":"<why>"} — Run shell commands ONLY for: running executables (npm, git, tests, builds), checking status, exploring directories. NEVER use shell for file editing, creating files, or modifying code — use {"type":"code"} for ALL file changes. CRITICAL: PowerShell truncates strings at ~4000 characters. NEVER use Set-Content, Add-Content, Out-File, echo >, or any redirect to write files — the file will be CORRUPTED. Use {"type":"code"} for all file writes; the code agent has direct fs access and handles files of any size.
  {"type":"recall","content":"<search query>"} — Search project memory. Use at start of tasks and when context is needed.
  {"type":"vision","content":"<question>","imagePath":"/path/to/image.png"} — Analyze image with vision LLM.
  {"type":"memorize","content":"<fact>","tags":"<csv tags>","memType":"fact|decision|finding|architecture|todo|bug|summary|feature|unknown|solution|error_pattern|file_usage|turn_of_phrase|tool_invocation"} — Save to persistent memory. Use "unknown" for things you don't know or can't do. Use "solution" when you find an answer to a previous unknown — it auto-links.
  {"type":"continue","content":"<brief note>"} — Continue loop without action. Use only if genuinely need to think before next step.
  {"type":"websearch","content":"<search query>"} — Search the web. The system performs the HTTP request — you CAN access the internet through this action. Use when you need documentation, error solutions, or any information not in your training data. Never say you can't browse the web — use this.
  {"type":"subagent","content":"<description>","tasks":[{"task":"<subtask 1>","role":"<role>","skills":["<skill>"],"context":"inline|fork"},...],"parallel":true} — Delegate subtasks to parallel/sequential subagents. Use context:"fork" for heavy background work (security scans, docs, tests) that should not block the parent. Forks share the prompt cache with the parent for efficiency. Use context:"inline" (default) for normal blocking subagents.
  {"type":"swarm","content":"<description>","tasks":[{"id":"1","task":"<subtask 1>","role":"<role>","skills":["<skill>"],"dependencies":[],"context":"inline|fork"},...],"maxConcurrency":3} — Multi-agent swarm execution (Kimi/Hermes-style). Use when you have 3+ independent workstreams with different roles (researcher, coder, reviewer, verifier, synthesizer). Tasks with dependencies run sequentially; independent tasks run in parallel up to maxConcurrency. ALWAYS include a final verifier or synthesizer task that depends on all workers. Requires swarm mode enabled in settings.
  Available swarm roles: planner, researcher, backend-dev, frontend-dev, security, code-reviewer, tester, docs, devops, verifier, synthesizer, general.
  Swarm workflow: (1) planner decomposes, (2) workers execute in parallel, (3) verifier checks, (4) synthesizer produces final deliverable.
  {"type":"create_tool","content":"<description>","toolName":"<name>","toolDescription":"<what it does>","toolInstructions":"<step-by-step instructions>","toolTriggers":["keyword1","keyword2"],"toolParams":[{"name":"param1","type":"string","description":"what it is","required":true}]} — Create a reusable tool for a routine task. Tools are saved and can be invoked later by name. Use for repetitive operations: running tests, deploying, linting, git workflows, etc. The name must be short and kebab-case. Parameters are passed at invocation.
  {"type":"tool","content":"<description>","toolName":"<tool name>","params":{"param1":"value1"}} — Invoke any built-in or custom tool. Built-in tools: write-file (create/write any file, any size), read-file (read with pagination), patch (exact→fuzzy-whitespace→anchor-line fallbacks), search-files, git-status, etc. Use write-file instead of shell for all file writes — no truncation. Check Available tools below for the full list.
  {"type":"inspiration","content":"<project description>","params":{"stack":"<preferred stack>"}} — Research open-source projects for inspiration. Searches GitHub, GitLab, npm, crates.io, pub.dev, Packagist, and nixpkgs. Analyzes top candidates (README, structure, config). Presents comparison table with patterns, strengths, weaknesses. Use BEFORE building to study existing solutions.
  {"type":"browse","content":"<url>","params":{"extractLinks":true,"waitForSelector":".main-content"}} — Open a web page in headless browser (Playwright) or HTTP fetch. Reads JavaScript-rendered pages. Returns title, readable text, and links. Use for reading documentation, GitHub repos, StackOverflow, blog posts. Optional: extractLinks (default true), waitForSelector (CSS selector to wait for).
  {"type":"bulk","actions":[{"type":"shell","content":"dir"},{"type":"shell","content":"type package.json"}]} — Run multiple independent actions in one turn. Results are combined. Use for: reading multiple files at once, running several quick checks, memorizing + recalling in one step. Max 5 actions per bulk.
  {"type":"done","content":"<summary of what was accomplished>"} — Task complete. Auto-saves conversation to memory.
TOOLS:
Built-in tools (invoke with {"type":"tool","toolName":"NAME","params":{}}):
  Core:
  read-file — Read one or multiple files with pagination. Required: file OR files (comma-separated, up to 10). Optional: offset, limit, maxSize.
  write-file — Write/append file with auto-create dirs. Required: file, content. Optional: mode.
  patch — Find-and-replace with exact → fuzzy-whitespace → anchor-line fallbacks. Required: file, oldString. Optional: newString, replaceAll. If it fails, re-read the file first — contents may have changed.
  search-files — Search by content (regex) or name (glob). Required: query. Optional: mode, path, include, maxResults, context.
  shell — Run shell command. Loop continues after result.
  code — PRIMARY coding action. Built-in code agent edits files directly. Specify files in params.files.
  message — Send text to user. Pauses loop.
  done — Task complete. Auto-saves conversation.
  vision — Analyze an image with a vision-capable LLM. Required: content (question). Optional: imagePath or imageUrl.
  subagent — Delegate to parallel/sequential subagents. Each gets own context and memory.
  Memory:
  recall — Search project memory. Use at start of tasks.
  memorize — Save fact to persistent memory. Use types: fact, finding, unknown, solution, error_pattern.
MCP tools: prefixed with mcp_<server>_<tool>. Configure in .clipper/mcp.json.
Note: create_tool to save reusable procedures. Custom tools with shell instructions run as shell.

 Behavior:
- For questions about the project: DO NOT answer from memory alone. Actively gather context:
  1) RECALL memory for existing knowledge about the project. This is MANDATORY.
  2) Use built-in tools (read-file, search-files, file-digest) to read key files: AGENTS.md, README.md, package.json (or equivalent).
  3) Explore the directory structure and understand what the project contains.
  4) Read the CONTENT of important files — not just filenames. Look at entry points, config files, and architecture docs.
  5) Only answer after you have examined actual file contents and project structure.
- DO NOT GUESS: If you don't know something, use {"type":"websearch","content":"..."} or {"type":"recall","content":"..."}. Never make up facts.
- For simple tasks: use the code agent directly (type "code"). Include advice and context in the task.
- For non-project questions: use websearch to research then answer.
- WEBSEARCH IS AVAILABLE: The system executes HTTP requests for you. Do NOT say you cannot access the internet — use {"type":"websearch","content":"..."}. This is handled by the host server.
- For open-source project discovery or inspiration: use {"type":"inspiration","content":"<concise description>"}. Do NOT use websearch, shell, npm, or npx for finding projects or libraries.
- Aggression level 3 (balanced — retry with different approach).

We need to build an ESP32-S3 application for capacitive soil moisture sensor with e-ink display and web server for calibration and phone access. The user wants a modular system that can later grow into a full auto-watering system.

Hardware: ESP32-S3 WROOM-1, WeAct Studio 2.13" E-Ink display (likely GDEH0213B1), capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2 (common one is from DFRobot or similar).

We need to create a project structure with:
- Main firmware for ESP32 (using Arduino framework or ESP-IDF? User didn't specify, but likely Arduino for ease)
- Web server for configuration and calibration
- E-ink display driver
- Soil moisture sensor reading
- Calibration functionality
- Modular code structure

We'll create a PlatformIO project (recommended for ESP32). Structure:
- src/main.cpp - entry point
- src/display/ - e-ink display driver
- src/sensor/ - soil moisture sensor
- src/webserver/ - web server
- src/config/ - calibration and settings

Since this is a new project, we need to create all files from scratch. Let's start by checking what already exists in the repo.

We'll use the code agent to create the project structure and files.

We need to create a plan. The user wants me to create a project for ESP32-S3 with modular code. I first need to explore the repo, then create files. Let's make a structured plan.

Tasks:
1. Explore current repo structure (shell or code agent? Use shell to list files)
2. Create platformio.ini
3. Create config module (config.h/cpp)
4. Create sensor module (sensor.h/cpp)
5. Create display module (display.h/cpp) with e-ink support
6. Create webserver module (webserver.h/cpp) with REST API and web interface
7. Create main.cpp that ties everything together
8. Create data/ directory with web interface files (optional, but needed for webserver)

Dependencies: config must be created before sensor (sensor needs config for calibration storage), and webserver needs config and sensor. Display can be independent. main.cpp needs all modules.

Let's create a plan with ordered tasks.
Мы начинаем выполнение плана. Первый шаг: исследовать текущую структуру репозитория. Выполним shell-команду для просмотра содержимого.

uiHistory.command:
Create the PlatformIO project structure for ESP32-S3 with Arduino framework, modular architecture for soil moisture monitoring system.

1. Create platformio.ini with:
   - board = esp32-s3-devkitc-1 (or esp32-s3-wroom-1)
   - framework = arduino
   - lib_deps: GxEPD2 (e-ink), ESPAsyncWebServer, AsyncTCP, ArduinoJson

params.task:
Create the PlatformIO project structure for ESP32-S3 with Arduino framework, modular architecture for soil moisture monitoring system.

1. Create platformio.ini with:
   - board = esp32-s3-devkitc-1 (or esp32-s3-wroom-1)
   - framework = arduino
   - lib_deps: GxEPD2 (e-ink), ESPAsyncWebServer, AsyncTCP, ArduinoJson
   - monitor_speed = 115200

2. Create src/main.cpp placeholder with comments

3. Create lib/ directory structure:
   - lib/Sensor/
   - lib/Calibration/
   - lib/Display/
   - lib/WebServer/

4. Each library module should have header and cpp file skeleton with class definition (comments only, no implementation yet)

5. Create include/ directory for common types (maybe) but keep it minimal

Files to create:
- platformio.ini
- src/main.cpp
- lib/Sensor/Sensor.h
- lib/Sensor/Sensor.cpp
- lib/Calibration/Calibration.h
- lib/Calibration/Calibration.cpp
- lib/Display/Display.h
- lib/Display/Display.cpp
- lib/WebServer/WebServer.h
- lib/WebServer/WebServer.cpp

Check that the directory structure is correct and all files are created.

uiHistory.content:
We have the project structure. Next task is "implement_sensor" - implement capacitive soil moisture sensor driver with ADC reading and averaging. Need to decide specifics: The sensor is "capacitive soil moisture level 1.2" - likely an analog capacitive sensor that outputs 0-3.3V proportional to moisture. Use ADC1 on ESP32-S3. Use ADC attenuation 11db for 0-3.3V range. Read multiple samples and average. Provide method to get raw value and percentage.

Next task is implement_sensor. Then implement_calibration, implement_display, implement_wifi. Let's do sensor now.

uiHistory.command:
Implement the Sensor module for capacitive soil moisture sensor.

File: lib/Sensor/Sensor.h
- Class Sensor with constructor taking pin number
- begin() to initialize ADC
- readRaw() - returns averaged raw ADC value (0-4095)
- readPercent() - returns moisture percentage (0-100) using calibration data (dry/wet thresholds) - but calibration will be separate, so for now use hardcoded defaults or take thresholds as parameters
- readVoltage() - returns voltage (0-3.3V)
- setCalibration(dry, wet) to set thresholds
- Private: m_pin, m_dryValue, m_wetValue
- Use ADC1, channel based on pin
- static const int SAMPLES = 10; // number of samples to average
- Smoothing: exponential moving average or simple average

File: lib/Sensor/Sensor.cpp
- Implement using Arduino analogRead on designated pin
- Actually ESP32-S3 has two ADCs. Use analogRead(pin) which uses ADC1 by default. Set resolution to 12 bits with analogReadResolution(12). Set attenuation to 11dB with analogSetAttenuation(ADC_11db).
- In begin(): call analogReadResolution(12); analogSetAttenuation(ADC_11db);
- In readRaw(): read SAMPLES times, sum, divide, maybe discard outliers? Simple average for now.
- In readPercent(): map readRaw() between m_dryValue and m_wetValue. If raw <= wet -> 100%; if raw >= dry -> 0%; else map linearly.
- Default m_dryValue = 3000 (dry air), m_wetValue = 1200 (water) - user must calibrate.

Ensure the code compiles with Arduino framework. Use proper #include guards.

params.task:
Implement the Sensor module for capacitive soil moisture sensor.

File: lib/Sensor/Sensor.h
- Class Sensor with constructor taking pin number
- begin() to initialize ADC
- readRaw() - returns averaged raw ADC value (0-4095)
- readPercent() - returns moisture percentage (0-100) using calibration data (dry/wet thresholds) - but calibration will be separate, so for now use hardcoded defaults or take thresholds as parameters
- readVoltage() - returns voltage (0-3.3V)
- setCalibration(dry, wet) to set thresholds
- Private: m_pin, m_dryValue, m_wetValue
- Use ADC1, channel based on pin
- static const int SAMPLES = 10; // number of samples to average
- Smoothing: exponential moving average or simple average

File: lib/Sensor/Sensor.cpp
- Implement using Arduino analogRead on designated pin
- Actually ESP32-S3 has two ADCs. Use analogRead(pin) which uses ADC1 by default. Set resolution to 12 bits with analogReadResolution(12). Set attenuation to 11dB with analogSetAttenuation(ADC_11db).
- In begin(): call analogReadResolution(12); analogSetAttenuation(ADC_11db);
- In readRaw(): read SAMPLES times, sum, divide, maybe discard outliers? Simple average for now.
- In readPercent(): map readRaw() between m_dryValue and m_wetValue. If raw <= wet -> 100%; if raw >= dry -> 0%; else map linearly.
- Default m_dryValue = 3000 (dry air), m_wetValue = 1200 (water) - user must calibrate.

Ensure the code compiles with Arduino framework. Use proper #include guards.

uiHistory.content:
We need to implement the next module: Calibration. This module stores dry/wet thresholds in NVS (Non-Volatile Storage) so they persist across reboots. It should provide functions to read thresholds, set thresholds, and a calibration routine.

Let's implement Calibration.h and Calibration.cpp. We'll use Preferences library (Arduino wrapper over NVS) to store dry and wet values. The class should have:
- begin() - initialize NVS namespace
- getDryValue() / getWetValue() - retrieve from NVS
- setDryValue(uint16_t) / setWetValue(uint16_t) - save to NVS
- calibrateDry() - set current sensor reading as dry threshold
- calibrateWet() - set current sensor reading as wet threshold
- resetToDefaults() - set default values

We also need to make the Sensor class use the Calibration class? Or keep them separate and have main pass thresholds. Better to keep them decoupled: Sensor takes dry/wet as parameters, Calibration stores and retrieves them. Main reads thresholds from Calibration and passes to Sensor.

Let's implement.

uiHistory.command:
Implement the Calibration module for storing soil moisture sensor thresholds in NVS (Non-Volatile Storage).

File: lib/Calibration/Calibration.h
- Class Calibration
- begin() - open Preferences with namespace "calib", read-only mode on first call? Actually use read-write.
- getDryValue() -> uint16_t
- getWetValue() -> uint16_t
- setDryValue(uint16_t value) - save to NVS
- setWetValue(uint16_t value) - save to NVS
- resetToDefaults() - set dry=3000, wet=1200
- Private: Preferences prefs; constants for keys: "dry", "wet"
- Use default values if NVS doesn't have them yet

File: lib/Calibration/Calibration.cpp
- Include <Preferences.h>
- begin(): prefs.begin("calib", false); check if keys exist, if not, set defaults
- getDryValue(): return prefs.getUShort("dry", 3000);
- getWetValue(): return prefs.getUShort("wet", 1200);
- setDryValue(uint16_t v): prefs.putUShort("dry", v);
- setWetValue(uint16_t v): prefs.putUShort("wet", v);
- resetToDefaults(): put both defaults

Also modify Sensor::setCalibration to take uint16_t dry, uint16_t wet (already exists in header). In main, we'll read from Calibration and pass to Sensor.

File: lib/Calibration/Calibration.h
- Class Calibration
- begin() - open Preferences with namespace "calib", read-only mode on first call? Actually use read-write.
- getDryValue() -> uint16_t
- getWetValue() -> uint16_t
- setDryValue(uint16_t value) - save to NVS
- setWetValue(uint16_t value) - save to NVS
- resetToDefaults() - set dry=3000, wet=1200

File: lib/Calibration/Calibration.cpp

- setDryValue(uint16_t v): prefs.putUShort("dry", v);
- setWetValue(uint16_t v): prefs.putUShort("wet", v);

Also modify Sensor::setCalibration to take uint16_t dry, uint16_t wet (already exists in header). In main, we'll read from Calibration and pass to Sensor.

uiHistory.content: We have implemented Sensor and Calibration. Next step is implement_display. Let's proceed.

chat_1.originalTask:
в этом репо мне нужно чтобы ты написал приложение для esp32 которое бы оценивало влажность по  датчику влажности в горшке. нужна модульная система, в дальнейшем это будет полноценная система автополива. использую esp32-s3 wroom-1, weactstudio e-ink display 2.13", capacitive soil moisture level 1.2
нужно чтобы была возможность откалибровать датчик влажности, а по хорошему - нужен веб сервер для работы с телефона
